explanation.text: Sali cannot afford the expensive cloth or sew the robe, so she invents work-for-payment arrangements with both the shopkeeper and tailor. Her sustained effort and practical solutions support the theme that determination and creative thinking overcome challenges.
The cloth is a glorious orange scrap that turns and dances in the hot wind. The comparison works because a flame is brightly colored and moves in a similar fluttering way.

Sali imagines the scrap as part of a flowing dress but decides to make it a lovely headpiece for her doll. This shows creativity in seeing different possibilities and thoughtfulness in sharing the treasure with the doll.

After earning the fabric, Sali realizes she cannot sew and asks herself how the robe will be made. These thoughts reveal that she is uncertain and has not yet formed a detailed plan for completing the project.

Both adults accept Sali's offer to work rather than dismissing a child who lacks money, and each gives her what she earns. Their willingness to make a fair exchange and help her achieve the robe shows generosity as well as fairness.

In paragraph 7, Sali offers to work for the shopkeeper to earn cloth; in paragraph 17, she offers the tailor work in exchange for sewing. These matching actions solve her problems because each trades labor for something she cannot buy.

The shopkeeper's statement connects Sali's many days of labor directly to receiving the expensive cloth. It supports the story's theme because her determination and hard work earn the reward she needs.

Janet wants to make a free-fall jump, but her father first refuses and says "Absolutely not" because he considers it dangerous. Their opposing views about whether she should parachute create the conflict.

Guthrie first joins a local sports-car club and begins entering and winning races, then an impressed driving instructor urges her toward auto racing. This sequence explains how casual driving developed into competitive success.

Saying it was "no surprise" that Guthrie excelled in a dangerous sport is an admiring judgment based on her lifelong love of adventure. Unlike the other factual details, it directly reveals the author's view of her daring character.

Just before a rare chance to test an Indianapolis-style car, Guthrie breaks a bone in her foot and receives a heavy cast. This injury is her biggest challenge because it threatens her ability to take the test and seize the opportunity.

From flying and parachuting to rebuilding engines and testing an unfamiliar race car with a broken ankle, Guthrie repeatedly seeks adventure. The opening statement best represents her because it describes the daring trait behind all those choices.

Guthrie calls the high-speed test a thrill immediately before comparing it with going to the moon. The simile refers to excitement because both experiences seem extraordinary and unlike ordinary travel.

The title emphasizes both speed and courage, qualities Guthrie shows by trying free-fall parachuting, competitive racing, and an unfamiliar championship car despite injury. These events support the idea that she fearlessly accepts thrilling new challenges.
"Trouble was brewing" is followed by falling temperatures, strong winds, and rapidly increasing snow. That context shows that a dangerous storm was developing.
The author compares air to a sponge that absorbs lake water and later wrings the moisture onto land. In that comparison, "sops up" means takes in the water.

When wind travels across more of the lake's surface, the air has more opportunity to absorb evaporated water. Thus, wind direction can increase the amount of moisture carried into the air and later released as snow.

Paragraph 7 says Buffalo residents experience massive snowstorms as a regular part of life and are well trained in snow removal. It describes local life because those details show how residents experience and handle frequent lake-effect snow.

The article explains that heat and moisture rise and cool before falling as snow, while the diagram also shows the clouds becoming larger during that process. The cloud-growth stage is therefore new information added by the visual.

The article's main purpose is to explain how lake-effect snow forms when below-freezing air passes over warmer lake water and gains moisture. That basic combination belongs in a summary because it states the phenomenon's central cause.

Lake-effect snow forms only when several factors align, including cold air, warmer open lake water, useful wind direction, and enough temperature difference. Because the article repeatedly explains those ingredients, the need for special conditions is its central idea.
